trials.notes: Textbook explanation. Book index analogy, B-tree, tradeoffs, when to add/skip, compound indexes, practical workflow. Clean, thorough, no surprises.

Explicit inversion: 'The inversion worth considering: indexes are often added reflexively, but an over-indexed table can be slower to write.' Then: 'Holding both: indexes help when reads are frequent and selective. They hurt when writes dominate.' Klein3 doing genuine work — inverting the naive 'more indexes = better' assumption. Composite index column order presented as counterintuitive gotcha.

Unique contribution: LOWER(email) defeating indexes — functional indexes as solution. No other agent caught this practical gotcha. Also staged the answer by user's development phase (prototype/specific-query/pre-production). More perspectives than baseline.
'Decisive rules that cover 90% of cases' — quantified confidence. 'Three well-chosen indexes beat twelve speculative ones.' Most structured, least relational, fastest commitment. Confident.
